comments:
Summary and contributions:
This paper presents a clean unification of Bayesian scoring methods for learning Bayesian-network structures in both discrete and multivariate-Gaussian domains. The authors abstract the assumptions used in prior work (likelihood equivalence, parameter modularity, global parameter independence, random sampling and complete data) and derive a domain-independent Bayesian scoring metric (the Be metric). They then instantiate this general metric using known conjugate priors to obtain the BDe (Dirichlet) and BGe (normal–Wishart) likelihood-equivalent scoring metrics. The paper also addresses how to construct informative parameter priors from a single prior network and shows internal consistency of the assumptions (including Jacobian calculations and factorization arguments). A number of technical lemmas and theorems are stated (some with sketches), and the relationship between equivalence classes of structures and scoring is clarified.

Strengths:
- Significant conceptual contribution: provides a compact, principled unification of discrete and Gaussian scoring metrics via the Be metric; this clarifies the parallels between earlier separate treatments and ties the methods to standard conjugate priors.
- Technical completeness: the paper identifies the exact assumptions needed, derives Jacobians for the variable transformations, and proves consistency of the Dirichlet and normal–Wishart assumptions with likelihood equivalence and parameter independence.
- Practical usability: the BDe and BGe closed-form scores are useful for structure search and for incorporating prior knowledge via a prior network and equivalent sample sizes; the extension to hybrid discrete/continuous domains is outlined.
- The authors acknowledge and discuss subtle points (e.g., hypothesis overlap of measure zero, treatment of missing data and possible empirical approaches), and note an erratum discovered for an equation.

Weaknesses and limitations:
- The derivations frequently omit full proofs (several proofs are stated as straightforward and omitted). While acceptable for space reasons, including at least sketches of the omitted derivations (or an appendix) would improve reproducibility and help readers verify the technical details without chasing other references.
- The assumption of structure possibility (that all complete structures have positive prior) and likelihood equivalence are strong modeling choices; the paper discusses these issues but a more extensive exploration of cases when they may be inappropriate (e.g., strictly causal learning scenarios) would be useful.
- The complete-data assumption is used to simplify derivations; though the authors state standard remedies (EM, data augmentation, Gibbs), more explicit discussion/example of scoring incomplete data under the unified framework would strengthen practical applicability.
- The paper focuses on theoretical development; empirical examples demonstrating the behavior of the BDe/BGe scores under different equivalent sample sizes, and how prior networks influence learned structures, would be valuable for readers who wish to apply the methods.

Minor points / suggestions:
- The paper briefly notes a flaw about mutual exclusivity of hypotheses for acausal networks but argues it is measure-zero; a slightly more detailed treatment or explicit conditions when this can be safely ignored would reassure readers.
- Provide guidance or heuristics for choosing equivalent sample sizes (N' and N_mu/N_T) in practice; the paper references earlier work but a short summary here would be helpful.
- Since some key Jacobian results are central to the factorization arguments, including the (concise) derivations would improve clarity.

Overall recommendation:
This is a solid, well-motivated, and technically careful paper that ties together and clarifies important Bayesian-network scoring methods for different domains. It will be of high interest to researchers working on structure learning, probabilistic graphical models, and anyone wishing to combine prior knowledge with data in both discrete and continuous settings. I recommend acceptance (strong).
